​@AlexandrusMegus here's why people see it as irresponsible:
Touching, riding, or posing with wild apex predators like sharks isn’t “conservation.” It stresses the animals, disrupts their natural behavior, and teaches people the wrong message that it’s safe or acceptable to treat wild creatures like pets or props.

And let’s be real: if Ocean truly cared about long-term shark protection, where’s the actual science? No peer-reviewed data in over 20 years? No transparent research shared with the public or scientific community? That’s not how conservation works that’s how content works.

This isn’t hate. It’s fair criticism. If she wants to be seen as a conservationist, then it has to be about the sharks not the camera always being on her.

​@juansharksWith all due respect, real conservation isn't about ego or who you “respect.” It’s about transparency, ethics, and impact. If Ocean truly cared about sharks, she’d share scientific data, follow wildlife handling standards, and work with marine biologists not ride and provoke wild animals for viral clips.

Criticism isn’t hate it’s accountability. And if her actions encourage risky, unscientific behavior, that’s harmful, not helpful. Awareness without ethics is just performance.

@medusa77733 Okay, fair point. I assume we can agree that there are plenty of shark conservationists out there who are not doing what you just listed. However, that doesn't prevent the death of 10.000 sharks every hour, and the majority of Earth's population still sees the sharks as mindless killing machines. I agree, apex predators (or any other animal on this planet), should be left to their own devices, but is she really making that much of an issue for sharks? From a certain point of view, she is doing something other than tagging and researching - she's trying to influence an average human who will NEVER EVER IN A MILLION YEARS read any peer reviewed paper on sharks, but will gladly watch a hot chick swimming with sharks and maybe, just maybe, change their opinion?

She’s not trying to normalize it—she’s showing people how to deter an assertive shark as a safety protocol in emergency situations. These are techniques she learned after sharks repeatedly pushed into her personal space as a kid spending so much time in the ocean. Over time, she figured out how to redirect that energy away from her.
She has never tried to ride a tiger shark. A long time ago—over a decade—she did grab onto a great white shark, purposely doing so to draw attention to their alarming extinction rates. At that time, no one cared about sharks, and the prevailing attitude was that 'the only good shark was a dead shark.'
The piece she did with GoPro in 2013 literally inspired millions to see sharks differently and became a massive win for shark conservation. People won’t protect what they see as monsters, but when you show that peaceful coexistence is possible, that’s a game-changer.
